rationale: The cited paper is used in the core theoretical setup of the citing article, not just in background listing. In the Fluency and Consumer Judgment section, the authors explicitly rely on Lee and Labroo (2004) as evidence that conceptual processing ease increases favorable product evaluations, and they use this fluency-to-liking logic to motivate their hypotheses in Studies 1 and 2. Although cited alongside other fluency papers, it supports a central mechanism the new paper builds on (unexpected ease or difficulty in customization decisions shaping preference).
relation_description: The citing paper treats Lee and Labroo (2004) as a key prior demonstration that conceptual fluency can improve brand evaluations and uses that mechanism as a foundation. It then advances the line by showing that in self-customization, fluency effects depend on discrepancies between expected and experienced difficulty.
